did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), specifically Shallow Learning (Resilient Backpropagation, One Step Secant) and Deep Learning (Convolutional Neural Networks with RMSProp, SGDM, ADAM optimizers) to classify eyelid basal cell carcinoma from facial photographs. The study achieved up to 100% accuracy using Deep Learning ANN for this dermatological classification task.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL PAPER' and reports original experimental results. It details the development and application of shallow and deep learning artificial neural networks to classify eyelid basal cell carcinoma from patient photographs, including specific methods, datasets, training algorithms, and performance metrics (accuracy scores).

title: Eyelid basal cell carcinoma classification using shallow and deep learning artificial neural networks
brief_summary: This study developed and evaluated shallow and deep learning artificial neural networks to classify eyelid basal cell carcinoma from facial photographs. The dataset consisted of 143 preprocessed grayscale images collected from the Clinic of Ophthalmology at the University Hospital in Heraklion, Crete, including cases of BCC and healthy subjects. The researchers employed shallow learning algorithms such as Resilient Backpropagation and One Step Secant, alongside deep learning Convolutional Neural Networks trained with RMSProp, SGDM, and ADAM optimizers. Performance was evaluated using accuracy scores, revealing that shallow learning methods achieved maximum accuracies below 65%. In contrast, deep learning models reached up to 100% accuracy with specific parameter settings, demonstrating their superior capability for this classification task. The authors conclude that deep learning ANNs are powerful tools for supporting medical diagnosis in dermatology, though they note the need for larger datasets in future work.

paper_terms: Basal Cell Carcinoma on the Eyelids
lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for classification. The dataset included 32 images of left-eye BCC and 33 images of right-eye BCC. The study focused specifically on eyelid BCC, noting it accounts for 10% of BCC cases which are located in head and neck areas.
The negative control class for classification. The dataset included 39 left-eye images and 39 right-eye images of healthy subjects. These were used to distinguish normal eyelid skin from BCC.

all_task_types.target: Eyelid Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) vs. Healthy/Normal Skin
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between pathological cases (eyelid BCC) and healthy subjects (normal skin) using facial photographs. The task involves pattern recognition and classification of discrete diagnostic categories (BCC vs. normal) using shallow and deep learning artificial neural networks. Performance was evaluated using accuracy scores (ratio of correctly classified cases).

datasets_used.name: University Hospital of Heraklion Eyelid BCC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of preprocessed grayscale facial photographs collected from the Clinic of Ophthalmology at the University Hospital in Heraklion, Crete, Greece. The images were split into right-eye and left-eye halves, resized to 200x200 pixels, and converted from RGB to grayscale. It includes images of patients with eyelid basal cell carcinoma and healthy subjects.

use.training: Used as input for training Shallow Learning (RBP, OSS, PRCG, FPCG) and Deep Learning (CNN with RMSProp, SGDM, ADAM) Artificial Neural Networks.
use.validation: Used for validation procedures of the Deep Learning ANN (Accuracy-Loss diagrams generated during training and validation).
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of the ANN performance (accuracy score calculated as ratio of correctly classified cases over total cases). Some images not used in training/validation were used to demonstrate classification of true negative and true positive cases.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the Artificial Neural Networks by calculating the ratio of correctly classified cases over the total number of cases.
To split the dataset into subsets for training and validation/testing to assess model generalization and prevent overfitting.
To visualize the evolution of model performance (accuracy) and error (loss) during the training and validation procedures.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy scores and classification error rates.

Resilient Backpropagation (RBP).details: Maximum accuracy achieved was 65% with 20 neurons in hidden layer and learning rate 0.1, 0.3, or 0.4. Other settings yielded accuracies between 40% and 62%.

One Step Secant (OSS).details: Maximum accuracy achieved was 60% with 20 neurons in hidden layer and learning rate 0.1. Other settings yielded accuracies between 40% and 55%.

Polak–Ribière Conjugate Gradient (PRCG).details: Maximum accuracy achieved was 65% with 20 neurons in hidden layer and learning rate 0.1 or 0.3. Other settings yielded accuracies between 45% and 60%.

Fletcher-Powell Conjugate Gradient (FPCG.details: Maximum accuracy achieved was 65% with 20 neurons in hidden layer and learning rate 0.1. Other settings yielded accuracies between 40% and 60%.

Shallow Learning ANN.overall_summary: Shallow Learning ANN did not manage to achieve classification performance more than 65%.

SGDM (Stochastic Gradient Descent with M.notes: Performance affected by parameter settings. With learning rate 0.02, accuracy lowered to 80%. With learning rate 0.04, accuracy decreased to 50%.

RMSProp (Root Mean Square Propagation).notes: One configuration reached 100% accuracy and zero classification error.

Deep Learning ANN.overall_summary: Deep Learning ANN reached up to 100% performance. Achieved performance seems to be affected by the parameter settings of the learning algorithm used.

Qualitative Results.true_negatives: A full-face photograph of a normal subject split into right and left halves was correctly classified as normal (true negative cases) by the trained Deep Learning ANN.
Qualitative Results.true_positives: A right-eye eyelid BCC case was correctly classified as pathological (true positive case) by the trained Deep Learning ANN.

architecture_used.main_model: Shallow Learning Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Deep Learning Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)

shallow_learning.hidden_layers: One hidden layer with varying neurons (10, 15, 20)

deep_learning.pooling: 2x2 max pooling with stride [2 2] and padding [0 0 0]

training_details.data_split: 70% training, 30% validation (for Deep Learning); 70% training, 15% validation, 15% test (for Shallow Learning)

classification_or_output_head.metric: Accuracy score (ratio of correctly classified cases over total cases)

preprocessing_techniques: Splitting full-face or half-face photographs into two halves corresponding to the right-eye and left-eye paraophthalmic regions
Resizing images to a uniform size of 200 × 200 pixels
Converting images from RGB color format to grayscale
conclusions: Deep learning artificial neural networks (ANN) are superior to shallow learning ANNs for classifying eyelid basal cell carcinoma from facial photographs, achieving up to 100% accuracy compared to a maximum of 65% for shallow learning methods.

The performance of deep learning models is highly sensitive to hyperparameter settings, particularly the learning rate, with specific configurations (e.g., RMSProp, ADAM, SGDM with specific learning rates and filter sizes) required to achieve optimal accuracy.

Deep learning ANNs can correctly classify unseen images of both healthy subjects (true negatives) and eyelid BCC patients (true positives), demonstrating their potential utility in supporting medical diagnosis.

The study acknowledges the limitation of using a small dataset (143 images) and recommends the use of larger datasets in future work to further validate and improve the generalization of the AI system.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small, single-center dataset of only 143 preprocessed grayscale images collected from one clinic (University Hospital of Heraklion, Crete), which limits the generalizability of the results.

Deep Learning models achieved up to 100% accuracy, but this performance was highly sensitive to hyperparameter settings (e.g., learning rate, number of filters, filtersize); small changes in parameters led to significant drops in accuracy (e.g., from 100% to 50% with SGDM).
Shallow Learning methods performed poorly, with maximum accuracies capped at 65%, suggesting that the dataset size is insufficient for effective training of shallow networks.
The paper does not report external validation; the model was evaluated only on the internal dataset split into training, validation, and testing subsets.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to assess the significance of the differences between model performances.

The dataset consists of preprocessed images (resized to 200x200 pixels, converted to grayscale) rather than raw clinical inputs, which may not reflect real-world variability in image quality and presentation.
The study acknowledges the need for larger datasets in future work to validate the AI system's utility in supporting medical diagnosis.
